issues.title: Is presenter a class 2 lead attester?
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
The presenter is proposed in the draft https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-rats-pkix-key-attestation/


Can we consider presenter as a class 2 lead attester, while the HSM is its connected component? As the selectively disclosed version: SD-CWT/SD-JWT of the Evidence of the HSM is then included as part of the Lead Attester's Evidence to it's Verifier.

issues.title: Clarity in taxonomy/figures
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
Thanks for adding figures. It was an interesting discussion in the design meeting today. Some issues that we could not address/discuss: 

- Figure should somehow clearly distinguish `role` from `entity` taking on that role. Mixing the two is confusing. One way to do it is [here](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10373038), i.e. dotted lines for `role` and solid lines for `entity`. Feel free to do it the way you like, or whatever is easy in the tool you use. My point is to distinguish the two.
- I believe the prefixes `local` and `remote` for verifier (e.g., for class 2) would be very helpful. 
- I would like to see precisely in which class the following real-world use cases lie: 
  - Arm CCA
  - Intel TDX + Nvidia GPU
If the taxonomy cannot represent the real-world use cases, then what's the point?

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body: @muhammad-usama-sardar ， that's a great question that gets to the heart of standards design: balancing current practice with future needs. I agree that known examples should be included without question. Where I see value is in also including categories that are logically necessary, even if not yet implemented. To keep things practical, we can label these as 'Reserved' to indicate their status. This way, we make the standard future-proof without compromising clarity. How does that sound to you?

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body:
> Where I see value is in also including categories that are logically necessary, even if not yet implemented. 

I feel that the "logically necessary" part needs a clear and strong justification. The technologies that actually exist (e.g., Arm CCA, Intel TDX + Nvidia GPU) should be given priority.

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body: One example is the A.2 in rfc9334, https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc9334.html#name-example-2-nonce-based-passp, I do not see too much real world implementation for this part, while logically it provides one method to prevent replay attack. I think this part is also related to the nonce discussion in this draft.

issues.title: add section about freshness, and nonce communication
issues.author: mcr
issues.body:
The flow of nonces from verifier(s) to attesting environments, for inclusion in evidence is inverse from the flow of evidence, but must preceed it.
A new set of diagrams may be necessary to explain what options there are for creation and verification of nonces.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
I think that it depends on the strategy of the Verifier. 

If Verifier sends a nonce to the Attesting Environment, and an expiration period is delivered inside the nonce, or is well-known, then the Attesting Environment needs to send the Evidence before the deadline, even B is absent. As there is a chance that the Verifier does not care about the status of the Attester B at all. 

If the attesting environment knows that the Verifier will reject the Evidence immediately if it is not complete, it needs to wait until B is available. As it cannot help to generate the Evidence from Attester B.

_Originally posted by @junzhanghw in https://github.com/mcr/composite-attesters/pull/10#discussion_r2428122732_

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
For the nonce for Evidence B/C, there are two options

option 1: the Attesting Environment, or another entity inside the Attester will forward the nonce from the Verifier. 

option 2: the Attesting Environment generates the nonce_e for B and C, when it gets the Evidence from them, it includes them in the data structure (claim, Evidence_B(nonce_e), Evidence_C(nonce_e), nonce, meta_data(nonce_e,...),signature). Then the Verifier needs to trust the attesting environment in a certain way.

_Originally posted by @junzhanghw in https://github.com/mcr/composite-attesters/pull/10#discussion_r2428134672_

issues.title: Consider the impact of live workload migrations
issues.author: TheBankster
issues.body:
One of many issues that will arise from workload migrations would be the impact of any credentials issued to a workload. E.g. if a workload is located in Germany and received a credential with a "location=Germany" claims, and is live-migrated to a server in France, that would necessitate either 1) figuring out a way of preventing the migration or 2) invalidation of any credential containing a now-invalid claim.
I'm sure other issues are also present.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: The claim/AR has to be linked to the hypervisor, and the location=XYZ would, I think, be an endorsement on the physical device.

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body: It may be mentioned as a security consideration.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> It may be mentioned as a security consideration.

It needs to be more than that.   When the workload is moved, it needs to break a chain of signatures.
So really, we need a way to tie the Evidence and/or AR of a component to the lead attester.
This also suggests that we need to be clearer about how a physical host and VM/container/TEE are connected as composites.    That was actually my goal in starting this document, but I lost track of that!

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body:
> we need to be clearer about how a physical host and VM/container/TEE are connected as composites.

That would be a valuable thing to do.

issues.title: concerns about sharing of nonces
issues.author: mcr
issues.body:
There are some circumstances where a Verifier could provide a challenge/response nonce to the Lead Attester A, and it would be appropriate for A to provide the same nonce to Components B and C for freshness.  
There some other situations where this would be a security violation.
The details of why/when need to be teased out.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: @gmandyam has some concerns.

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body:
There are some potential attacks

1. The lead Attester A refuses to forward the nonce.
2  The Lead Attester A changes the nonce.
3. The lead Attester A caches and forwards one old nonce to component B and C.

These three attacks has similar effect, that the external Verifier does not receive the expected Evidence for the component B and C.  One way to mitigate this is to attest the code of the Lead Attester A, and send the nonce in together with the signature from the Verifier.

issues.title: Draft Review Comments from Antoine Poulain
issues.author: yogeshbdeshpande
issues.body:
In Terminology:
    - in Local verifier: appraisal is done according to an Apprasial policy for Evidence ?
    But which entity drafts this policy ? Who is the Verifier Owner of a Local Verifier ?
    - "Le Grande Verificateur" in should be "Le Grand Verificateur" (masculine adjective in French)
    Same in "Acknowledgements", "La Grand" should be "Le Grand".
In Connectors -> Chaining:
    - Specify the trust relation between A and B. Like B is a target environment for A ?
    - What is meant by adjacent ? Does it means that there exist a physical communication path between the components ?
In Connectors -> Router:
    - Router is used for transit when two components are not "adjacent" ? To create a "route" ?
In Connectors -> Collection:
    - CM acronym not introduced for Conceptual Message.
    - provide a small explanation about what is meant by binding when talking about collections and
        what it is used for. It is mentioned in "Class 1" section that the binding is used to secure the message. But how does a binding secures a message when the binding is something else than a signature ?
    - provide small explanation to state how each binder presented can be used for binding.
In Class 0 diagram:
    - boot rom is mesured ? what is boot rom here ? is it the boot rom of the RTM ? if yes, this measurement is not needed right (there may be an endorsement for it). 
In Class 1 diagram:
    - looks like the class1.svg diagram has a problem: the lead attester is not represented correctly.
In class 2:
    - The Verifier is not a target environment for an attesting environment ? An AR from a verifier can only be trusted once the Verifier has been verified no ?
In Class 2 diagram:
    - it is written Evidence(Target A) and not Evidence(Attester A) like in class 1 diagram. Why this difference ?
In Class 3P:
    - "It [the Lead Attester's Verifier] needs to consider if the component has been assessed by a Verifier it trusts"
    You mean that the Lead verifier trusts the component Verifier or that the component trusts the component Verifier ?
    In the first case, where is this trust relation specified ? Is it in the Lead attester's Appraisal Policy for Evidence ?
Class 4:
    - Why is there a need for one of the attesting to be a Primary and the other a Secondary ?
    Can they bot be at the same "level" and each collect some claims which in the end form
    a complete claim set for the target environment ? In the Class 4 diagram, it is shown that
    the Primary attesting environment relays partial evidence to the Secondary one. So I understand that these attesting environments may have different capabilities and therefore should be distinguished.
    But can there also be a case where attesting environment 1 and attesting environment 2 are working on the same level, each producing partial evidence for the same target environment and both relaying their partial evidence to a Verifier ?

Overall: 
    - RATS work is pretty vast for new comers therefore (attestation/RATS-related) acronyms used in the document should always be explicited at least once (CM, LAK, RP) even if it is obvious for readers already familiar with RATS.
    - Whenever there is a diagram, there should be a corresponding description that links the scenario represented in the diagram with the named entities (A, B, C, RP, etc.) visible in the diagram.
    - Proposition: (if needed) add basic trust assumptions depending on the composite device class
        (e.g., there must be at least an attesting environment with an RTM, etc.).
    - Proposition: add concrete examples of the structure of CoRIM/Evidence documents/collection corresponding to composite Attester structure.

issues.title: Use of nonce terminology should be qualified
issues.author: nedmsmith
issues.body:
Considering the intended audience broadly covers mutiple disciplines, readers might be confused as to which type of freshness "nonce" mechanism is intended. Attestation introduces a novel context (evidence freshness) vs. existing contexts. I assert there are three highly distinct context where "nonce" terminology is used.
* Protocols - the primary objective is to detect replay of a protocol packet, frame, or message.
* Cryptography - the primary objective is to prevent various types of cryptology attacks such as [ECDSA side-channel](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-13176)
* Attestation - the primary objective is to control claims collection timing (when the AE actually collects new claims and / or invalidates cached claims)

The discussion on 2026-02-16 determined the best way to capture this topic was to add a discussion about the three types of nonce to [draft-ietf-rats-interaction-models](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-rats-reference-interaction-models/). This draft should reference the interaction models draft to establish terminology and foundational concepts. 

There was a comment that RFC9334 should have done more to capture the notion that there is a RATS Conceptual Message flow from Verifier to Attester that embodies the claims collection control information. If/when there is an update to 9334, it seems to make sense to incorporate this additional type of conceptual message.

From the perspective of composite attesters, the assumed singleton freshness conceptual message that flows from Verifier/RP to Attester can be replcated across multiple Attesters given compositions that replicate or divide the Attester role across multiple entities (i.e. Actors).  This draft should not seek to redefine the semantics of a "freshness conceptual message" (a.k.a. "attestation nonce"), but to consider composition dynamics such as replication, timing, and conveyance challenges stemming from richer compositions.

comments.author: stuyod01
comments.body:
I still don't understand how for the attestation nonce that the primary objective is to control claims collection timing.

When a Verifier requests attestation evidence from an Attester it provides a nonce so that it knows that the signed set of claims   it receives as evidence are fresh.  In the case of a TPM it doesn't matter whether the PCRs were last extended into 2 years ago, what matters is that freshness of the TPM quote.  This is to prevent replay attacks.

comments.author: nedmsmith
comments.body: You're conflating two uses of nonces. A protocol nonce protects against replay even when the claims are stale. An attestation nonce tells the AE to collect fresh claims.

comments.author: stuyod01
comments.body: Ok, so in the attestation scenario I gave, the nonce sent by the Verifier to ensure freshness of claims is a "protocol" nonce even though it was in an attestation scenario?  Is that right?

issues.title: Mapping attestation scenarios into classes
issues.author: stuyod01
issues.body:
**Scenario #1: Basic firmware attestation scenario with a TPM:**

- During the boot of an SoC various multiple stages of firmware components record measurements of images and security state into TPM PCRs and produce an event log
- At a later point in time a remote Verifier requests an attestation of the system. A user space daemon requests a PCR quote from the TPM and sends the quote and event log as evidence to the Verifier

Is this a composite attester? Which class? Class 1 seems to be the closest, but I don't see the clear mapping to Class 1. There are multiple attesting environments, but their measurements are all combined in the TPM. Is the user space daemon a "Lead Attester"?  The daemon doesn't produce any evidence of its own or sign anything. The daemon doesn't wrap the TPM quote as suggested in the Class 1 diagram.

**Scenario #2: Basic firmware attestation + user space**

- Same scenario as #1, but with additional kernel and user space components
- In the OS, systemd uses PCRs 8-15 and NVIndexes to measure kernel, kernel command line, initrd, etc. It has a separate event log.
- In the OS, IMA (Integrity Measurement Architecture) uses PCR10 for filesystem measurements. It has a separate event log.
- At a later point in time a remote Verifier requests an attestation of the system.

Is this a composite Attester? Which class?

**Scenario #3: Basic firmware attestation + PCIe device SPDM attestation**

- Same scenario as #1, but with system firmware requesting attestation claims from SPDM capable PCIe devices and measurements of the SPDM measurements being extended into PCRs.

Which class is this?

**Scenario #4: Basic firmware attestation with a TPM, plus SoC root-of-trust attestation**

- Same scenario as #1, but in addition to the firmware boot chain, there is an on-die root-of-trust enclave (e.g. Arm RSE) that holds measurements of some firmware components plus security state.  It provides a separate attestation (EAT token) that is distinct from the TPM quote.
- Which class is this?

reviews.body:
I rewrote one paragraph based upon my understanding.
Since you called one X, the other has to be Y?
Let's avoid AE a new term for now?

comments.body:
```suggestion
Example of one such system is a CPU system of a desktop from a Vendor X, which has its built in Attesting Environment, integrated into a product Y which requires a mandatory TPM support. 
In such situations one can anchor the Roots of Trust of Vendor X's CPU Attestation using a secondary 
Attesting Environment with the TPM Attestation. 
Alternatively, generate a TPM Quote and anchor it to Roots of Trust  CPU Attestation based of Vendor X's Attesting Environment
```

option 2: the Attesting Environment generates the nonce_e for B and C, when it gets the Evidence from them, it includes them in the data structure (claim, Evidence_B(nonce_e), Evidence_C(nonce_e), nonce, meta_data(nonce_e,...),signature). Then the Verifier needs to trust the attesting environment in a certain way.

https://www.cncf.io/blog/2025/10/08/a-tpm-based-combined-remote-attestation-method-for-confidential-computing/

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
Here, attesting environment 2 has to verify and/or trust attesting environment 1's signature.
It does not verify the evidence, it verifies the origin.
(This is a chain of custody problem)

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body:
> Here, attesting environment 2 has to verify and/or trust attesting environment 1's signature. It does not verify the evidence, it verifies the origin. (This is a chain of custody problem)

I think in such a case, it is very similar to the class 1. The only difference is that, the attestation enviornment 2 does not include the local claim.

```suggestion
There is an assumption that the Attesting Environment 1 (AE1) "trusts" Attesting Environment 2 (AE2), which means that AE2 has to verify the signature from AE1, otherwise AE2 can become a "signing fool".
This verification can be based upon a local credential.
```

```suggestion
Example of one such system is a CPU system of a desktop from a Vendor X, which has its built in Attesting Environment, integrated into a product Y which requires a mandatory TPM support. (EDIT: This example to be clarified)

```

pulls.title: Update acknowledgments for Jun Zhang's contributions
pulls.author: junzhanghw
pulls.body: Expanded acknowledgment of Jun Zhang's contributions.

comments.body:
```suggestion
Local Verifier:
: When an Attesting Environment *appraises* Evidence from another Attesting Environment, then it operates as a Local Verifier.  Mere examination of the signature on the Evidence (perhaps using a local credential) is not appraisal.

Local Validation:
: in some classes, Evidence is passed around, and must remain integral.  Local Validation involves checking the authenticity of the end-point.   This could involve a signature, or require physical security of that end-point.
```

comments.body:
```suggestion
For Class 3P, the Lead Attester is responsible for communicating with each of the Component Verifier. As a result, the specific freshness protocol of each of the Component system may differ. In the End the Lead Attester may present a claim to the Lead Verifier that indicates that freshness of individual components have been Verified.
```
